system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 04:08 PM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
Law Firm Wiki compiler — Batch 1 of 3. You are compiling PI practice knowledge from case activity logs into wiki articles.

1. Read /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md for the full compilation schema
2. Read ALL existing articles in /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
3. For each case in /tmp/batch_1_cases.txt (66 cases), read the activity logs in cases/<slug>/Activity Log/. For cases with many logs (>50 files), sample the first 10 and last 10 chronologically.
4. UPDATE existing articles: increment evidence_count, upgrade confidence (5+ cases = medium, 10+ = high), add new evidence bullets
5. CREATE new articles only for genuinely new patterns not covered by existing articles
6. ANONYMIZE all PII — use 'Case A', 'Case B', or descriptive patterns
7. Write articles to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
8. Append a compilation summary to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md
9. Do NOT rewrite wiki/index.md — the parent will rebuild it after all batches

Focus on extracting: settlement patterns, negotiation tactics, treatment timelines, SOL management, adjuster behavior, lien resolution, demand strategies.
I'll start by reading the compilation schema, the batch file, and existing articles in parallel.
messages.reasoning: Let me start by reading the compilation schema and understanding the structure, then read existing articles, and finally process the batch of cases.

# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

# Adjuster Reassignment Patterns

> Insurance carriers frequently reassign adjusters during the life of a PI claim, sometimes multiple times. Each reassignment resets the negotiation relationship and often causes delays as the new adjuster reviews the file from scratch. In long-running cases, the firm may interact with 3-5 different adjusters on a single claim. Understanding this pattern helps manage expectations and maintain negotiation momentum.

## Pattern

Adjuster reassignment follows observable patterns:

1. **Initial Adjuster**: Handles the claim from opening through liability investigation. May make the first offer.
2. **Mid-Case Reassignment**: Common at 6-12 months. May coincide with carrier restructuring or adjuster caseload rebalancing.
3. **Stale File Reassignment**: When a case has had no movement for extended periods, carriers may reassign to a "closer" adjuster who proactively reaches out to resolve.
4. **New Adjuster Introduction**: New adjusters typically introduce themselves, note they've been "reassigned" to the file, and request updated documentation.
5. **Fresh Eyes Effect**: A new adjuster sometimes brings movement — they re-evaluate the file without the baggage of prior negotiations and may make a new offer that differs from the prior adjuster's position.

### Impact on Negotiations

- Each reassignment adds 2-4 weeks of delay as the new adjuster reviews the file
- New adjusters may not honor informal commitments made by prior adjusters
- A new adjuster may request documentation already provided to the prior adjuster
- However, reassignment can be an opportunity: the new adjuster may be more willing to negotiate than a predecessor who was entrenched in a position

## Evidence

- In Case D, the BI claim went through at least 3 adjusters over 2+ years:
  - Adjuster 1 (initial): Handled the claim during the liability investigation phase, while coverage was still "under review"
  - Adjuster 2: Made the initial offer. After receiving updated records, the claim was "reassigned to a different adjuster" with the hope it would "cause some movement."
  - Adjuster 3: Self-identified as "the new adjuster on this file." Left multiple voicemails proactively reaching out, noting "there really has not been any movement" and "I was hoping to... try to follow up." This adjuster made multiple attempts to reach the attorney over several weeks before connecting.
  - Adjuster 4: Subsequently reassigned again; the new adjuster reviewed additional treatment records and made the carrier's "top offer."
- The Adjuster 3 reassignment was particularly notable: this adjuster was proactive, called multiple times, left detailed voicemails expressing willingness to negotiate, and explicitly said "I think that if we could have a phone conversation about this, we could get it resolved."
- After submitting updated records, the carrier reassigned to Adjuster 4, who raised the offer but also introduced a new defense (attributing treatment to an unrelated slip-and-fall incident).
- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the UIM claim went through multiple adjuster reassignments at a national insurance carrier. The PIP adjuster, BI adjuster, and UIM adjuster were all separate individuals at different carrier entities. Over the 3+ year case timeline, at least 3-4 adjusters handled the UIM claim track alone. This confirms that adjuster reassignment is not limited to BI claims — UIM adjusters are also subject to frequent reassignment, and the pattern of multiple adjusters per claim track compounds in multi-carrier cases.
- In an auto accident case (DOI 12/2022, KY), the PIP and UM claims were managed through the same carrier but the case saw 7 named adjusters across all tracks (PIP, BI, and UM), including adjusters from the carrier's parent company. The UM adjuster managed the claim through active litigation including discovery completion. This is the highest adjuster count observed per case and confirms that complex multi-track cases generate the most adjuster churn.
- In a small MVA case (DOI 11/2025, KY, file setup phase), 3 different adjusters were named on the case within the first few months: the BI adjuster (Auto Owners Insurance), the PIP adjuster (Progressive), plus an initial BI contact who was replaced before any substantive communication occurred. The case also had the PIP adjuster request recorded statements from both the client and a companion passenger as part of a "proof of loss" investigation. This confirms that adjuster reassignment can happen before the negotiation phase — even during file setup — and that cases with split BI/PIP carriers inherently involve multiple adjuster relationships from day one.
- In a companion case pair (DOI 11/2025, KY), both cases shared the same BI adjuster (State Farm) and the same PIP team (State Farm centralized PIP team at a dedicated extension). The PIP team was distinct from the BI adjuster, confirming the "PIP team" model where PIP adjusting is handled by a centralized team rather than an individual adjuster. This creates a different reassignment dynamic: individual BI adjusters may change, but the PIP team remains constant.

## Agent Guidance

1. When a new adjuster introduces themselves, log their name, direct phone, extension, and email immediately.
2. Proactively send the new adjuster a summary of the case status and any prior correspondence/offers.
3. Treat a new adjuster as an opportunity — respond promptly to their outreach.
4. If a new adjuster calls and leaves a voicemail, return the call within 24 hours (they are motivated to work the file).
5. Keep a chronological log of all adjusters who have handled the claim.
6. When the new adjuster requests documentation, send it promptly rather than assuming they have the prior adjuster's files.
7. Alert the attorney when an adjuster change occurs, as this may be a strategic opportunity to re-engage.

## Pitfalls

- Ignoring a new adjuster's outreach (especially when they are proactively trying to settle)
- Assuming the new adjuster has all documentation from the prior adjuster
- Not updating contact information when a reassignment occurs
- Missing the window of opportunity when a "closer" adjuster is assigned and motivated
- Letting weeks pass without responding to a new adjuster's repeated outreach

- [[client-expectations-management]] — adjuster changes add to client frustration about delays
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — adjusters may reference the approaching SOL as urgency to settle

# Adjuster Persistent Unreturned Calls Pattern

> Insurance adjusters sometimes repeatedly call the firm, leaving multiple voicemails over weeks or months without getting a return call. This pattern can occur on both sides: adjusters trying to settle may be unable to reach the attorney, or the firm may deliberately deprioritize a carrier's calls while focused on other aspects of the case. Both directions carry risk.

## Pattern

Two distinct patterns emerge:

### Adjuster-Initiated Persistence
When an adjuster repeatedly calls and leaves voicemails "to discuss settlement," this may indicate:
1. The adjuster has settlement authority and wants to close the file
2. The carrier is under pressure to resolve the claim
3. The adjuster's repeated calls create a documented record of their attempts

### Firm Non-Response
When the firm does not return adjuster calls promptly, this may be because:
1. The attorney is focused on litigation with a different carrier for the same case
2. The case is not ready for negotiation on that particular claim
3. The calls are being logged as "missed calls" without a follow-up task being created
4. Staff turnover means no one is monitoring that specific carrier's communications

## Evidence

- In Case D, the Progressive Insurance adjuster called the attorney repeatedly over a 3-month period (August through October 2025), leaving 7+ voicemails. Each voicemail was transcribed and logged as a "missed call" in Filevine, but no return calls were documented during this period. The voicemails progressively indicated the adjuster wanted "to discuss settlement" and "to talk this one over." Meanwhile, the firm was actively engaged in litigation against a different defendant (West Bend) on the same case. Eventually, the attorney did engage in email correspondence with this adjuster about the claim. The pattern suggests that when a firm is managing multiple parallel tracks, some carriers' outreach can fall through the cracks.

## Agent Guidance

1. Log every adjuster voicemail with the transcription and create a follow-up task
2. Even if the case is not ready for negotiation on that claim, return calls within 48 hours with a brief status update
3. Track the number of unreturned adjuster calls — more than 3 should trigger an alert to the attorney
4. When an adjuster says they want to "discuss settlement," this is a priority callback
5. Not responding to adjuster outreach can result in the adjuster closing the file or reducing their authority
6. If the firm is deliberately delaying engagement with a carrier for strategic reasons, document the attorney's direction to do so

## Pitfalls

- Letting adjuster calls go unreturned for weeks (damages the relationship and may reduce settlement authority)
- Not creating follow-up tasks from missed call logs (the pattern just accumulates without action)
- Assuming the adjuster will keep calling forever (they may close the file or reassign)
- Missing the signal that the adjuster is ready to settle while the firm is focused on litigation elsewhere
- Not distinguishing between strategic non-response (attorney-directed) and operational failure (nobody noticed)

## Related

- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — persistent adjuster calls should be escalated to the attorney
- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — adjuster may cite non-communication as a factor
- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — multiple carriers means more calls to track

# Attorney Review Triggers

> Certain events in a PI case require the attorney's direct involvement and approval. These are decision points where paralegal-level handling is insufficient and attorney judgment is needed. Identifying and routing these triggers efficiently prevents bottlenecks and ensures quality control.

## Pattern

Attorney review is triggered by:

1. **Initial Case Evaluation**: When a transfer case file arrives, the attorney reviews the prior firm's work, demand status, and financial picture before directing next steps
2. **Stale Case Flag**: When the attorney notices no activity on a case for an extended period (2+ months), they direct staff to check in with the client
3. **Lien Disputes**: When unrelated charges appear on a health insurance lien, the attorney directs which items to dispute
4. **Settlement Authority**: Client gives authority to "negotiate to LBF" (Last Best and Final) — the attorney must approve the negotiation range
5. **Case Complexity Flags**: Pre-settlement funding obligations, minimal treatment, incarcerated clients — these require attorney guidance on strategy
6. **Demand Approval**: Before a demand is sent, the attorney reviews and approves
7. **File Completion Escalation**: When records collection stalls, the attorney escalates to ensure action
8. **Litigation Decision**: When initial offers are inadequate (e.g., BI offer considering only ER visit despite extensive treatment), the attorney must decide whether to file suit
9. **IME Coordination**: When defense orders Independent Medical Examinations, the attorney must review the orders, advise the client, and manage scheduling
10. **Concurrent Claim Strategy**: When a client has injuries from multiple accidents, the attorney must decide apportionment strategy across claims

## Evidence

- In Case A, the attorney reviewed the transfer case and identified key issues: prior demand already sent, treatment gap, pre-settlement funding, and minimal treatment. He then directed staff on next steps.
- In Case B, the attorney flagged the case noting "no activity since the middle of August" and directed a check-in with the client. Later, the attorney changed the phase from Treatment to Demand in Progress.
- In Case C, the attorney identified that unrelated cardiac treatment was included in the Anthem lien and directed its removal. He also escalated when records requests had "no action" for a month.
- In Case D, the attorney intervened at multiple points in a VA lien case: (1) directed paralegal to sort VA records for "relatedness" to separate accident-related from unrelated treatment; (2) asked "What is going on here? Just need chiro records and can send demand" when records collection stalled; (3) ordered "prepare and send the demand" when the case had been pending for over 2 years, making a judgment call to proceed with incomplete VA records; (4) discussed the case with the client directly after the attorney call revealed "multiple other MRIs" and PT through the VA's IPRP program not in the file.
- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), the attorney directed: additional MRI imaging based on IME findings, IME amendment requests when the client's shoulder reached MMI, neurosurgery consultation, economist/vocational report ordering, and managed all litigation events (motions, mediation scheduling, discovery responses). The attorney also directly communicated with defense counsel on mediation logistics and discovery matters.
- In Case E (auto accident), the attorney flagged that there had been "NO attempted contact with client since December" and directed staff to check in. Later, the attorney personally called the adjuster and sent a lien payment request to the carrier. The attorney also had to reverse a premature phase change (Settlement -> Negotiation) when the client refused the offer.
- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the attorney was involved at multiple decision points: (1) deciding to file suit after West Bend's initial offer only considered the ER visit despite extensive treatment, (2) directly handling negotiation emails with Progressive's adjuster, (3) coordinating with defense counsel on IME scheduling and deposition dates, (4) responding to a provider's "case status inquiry" about payment prospects, and (5) directing the paralegal to "make sure records/bills in dropbox match what we have." The attorney's direct involvement with opposing counsel increased markedly once litigation was filed.
- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), the attorney: (1) called the client to discuss the first settlement offer (9/2023), (2) noted the client had "high expectations" wanting $10K+ net, (3) directed staff to follow up on the Anthem lien and check ZipLiens, (4) directed updated dental records requests after continued treatment, (5) forwarded client voicemails to the associate for return calls during litigation. The attorney delegated deposition scheduling to the associate but stayed involved in strategic decisions.
- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), the attorney: (1) personally called the client to discuss the case and provided a detailed call summary, (2) directed UIM complaint filing, (3) sent courtesy copy of the UIM complaint to the adjuster, and (4) escalated when records requests stalled. The attorney's direct call to the client, with a written summary sent to the case file, demonstrates a best practice for attorney-client contact.
- In Case H (auto accident, coverage denial), the attorney: (1) engaged directly with the BI adjuster to challenge the stolen vehicle coverage denial, providing evidence the vehicle wasn't reported stolen until after the accident, (2) made the litigation decision based on a "Litigation Review" when coverage remained unresolved, (3) granted defense counsel a 60-day extension to answer while they attempted to get coverage accepted, (4) agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC, and (5) discussed deposition scheduling strategy with the paralegal team. This case required more attorney involvement at the intake/file setup stage than typical cases due to the coverage dispute.

## Agent Guidance

1. Route the following to the attorney immediately:
   - New transfer case files received (with summary memo)
   - Settlement offers received
   - Lien itemizations with potential disputes
   - Client requests for case status update (if case has been inactive 30+ days)
   - Bills going to collections
   - Pre-settlement funding documentation
2. When routing to the attorney, include a brief summary and recommended action
3. Track attorney-pending items and follow up if no response within 48 hours
4. Document the attorney's direction in the activity log verbatim

## Pitfalls

- Not routing complex issues to the attorney (trying to handle them at paralegal level)
- Routing too many routine items to the attorney (creating bottlenecks)
- Not including sufficient context when routing (attorney has to dig through the file)
- Not following up on items pending attorney review
- Not documenting the attorney's direction

# Case Decline and Closing Workflow

> Not all retained cases proceed to demand and settlement. Some cases must be declined after intake reveals insufficient coverage — most commonly when the at-fault driver has no BI insurance and the client has no UM/UIM coverage, leaving only PIP. Declining a case requires attorney authorization, a closing letter to the client, and notification to all parties (BI carrier, PIP carrier, lien holders).

## Pattern

### Evaluation Triggers for Decline
1. **No BI coverage**: At-fault driver is uninsure

7. **Settlement Integration**: The VA lien must be satisfied from settlement proceeds. The VA expects "full reimbursement" plus any additional new-money settlement.

### Key Differences from Private Health Insurance Liens

| Aspect | Private Health Insurance | VA Lien |
|--------|------------------------|---------|
| Legal basis | State subrogation law / ERISA | Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651) |
| Contact | Claims/subrogation department | OGC Revenue Law Group (Paralegal Specialist) |
| Tracking | Claim number | GCL case number |
| Response time | Days to weeks | Weeks to months |
| Records access | Via provider records dept | Must go through VA facility; veteran can also use MyHealtheVet portal |
| Negotiation | Percentage reductions common | Federal government — less flexibility |
| Case lifecycle | Stays open until resolved | Can be closed and must be reopened |

## Evidence

- In Case D, the initial lien request was mailed to VA Healthcare on day 1 of file setup (July 2023). It took until July 2024 — a full year — before the VA's Revenue Law Group Paralegal Specialist responded with the lien amount of $4,437.13. During that year, multiple follow-up requests were required.
- The VA case was then closed in August 2024 when PIP paid the VA lien. However, when additional VA treatment was discovered later, the firm had to request the case be reopened. The VA case manager asked for "a list of dates you are including" before reopening.
- In Case Q (auto accident, VA patient, DOI 5/2023), the VA CCN - Optum lien was confirmed at $86,505.91 — the largest VA lien observed. The case involved air ambulance ($82K+), university hospital ($37K+), and extensive physical therapy through VA facilities. The firm contacted VA CCN - Optum directly and corresponded with the VA OGC regarding the lien. Even after filing a complaint and entering active litigation, the VA lien remained unresolved months later. The attorney continued calling Optum and writing to the OGC paralegal specialist. The case confirms that VA liens can dwarf private lien amounts and that resolution can take years when concurrent litigation is pending.
- The firm was unable to provide treatment dates without first obtaining the records, creating a circular dependency: the VA wanted dates to reopen; the firm needed records to identify dates. The paralegal pushed back: "We are unable to provide a list of dates related without the requested records to review."
- The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs for $4,437.13 upon receiving direction to pay along with the VA billing statement.
- The attorney noted the case was fundamentally complicated by VA involvement: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
- The BI adjuster (who was himself an Air Force veteran) recognized the VA lien dynamics in his voicemail, noting the VA "wants their money back" and structured his new-money offer around the VA balance.
- In a WC+BI case (DOI 6/2021, KY), the client received treatment at a VA Medical Center (James H. Quillen VA Medical Center). VA records were requested in July 2024 and received by July 2024. The case was in litigation for the BI claim while simultaneously managing a $30,926 WC lien and needing VA records. This case demonstrates that VA treatment records are required even in cases where the VA is not asserting a lien — the records are needed for demand preparation and litigation regardless. The VA facility's records collection added another dimension to an already complex dual-track (WC+BI) case.

## Agent Guidance

1. At intake, ask if the client receives any medical care through the VA. If yes, flag the case as "VA lien likely."
2. Send the initial lien request to VA Healthcare by mail during file setup — do not delay.
3. When the VA OGC Revenue Law Group assigns a case manager, record their name, phone, email, and the GCL case number in the case file.
4. All future correspondence must reference the GCL case number in the subject line.
5. Do NOT use veteran PII in unencrypted email to VA — reference GCL number only.
6. Do NOT send billing requests to the new-case intake fax line — that is for new cases only. Use the assigned case manager's direct contact.
7. Track VA records and VA billing as separate requests with separate follow-up timelines.
8. Set follow-up reminders at 30-day intervals — VA response times are measured in months, not days.
9. When PIP is available, consider directing PIP to pay the VA lien directly as a way to resolve the lien early.
10. If the VA case is closed and additional treatment is discovered, immediately contact the case manager with the list of new treatment dates to request reopening.
11. Advise the veteran client to access their own records via MyHealtheVet (https://www.myhealth.va.gov/) as a parallel path to obtain records faster.
12. When sorting VA records, have a paralegal review for "relatedness" to the accident — VA records will include all treatment, not just accident-related care.

## Pitfalls

- Expecting VA lien resolution to move at private-insurer speed (it does not — plan for months)
- Sending records requests to the wrong VA fax number (billing fax vs. records fax vs. intake fax)
- Not recording the GCL case number — all correspondence requires it
- Including veteran PII in unencrypted emails to VA
- Assuming the VA lien amount is final without checking for additional treatment dates
- Not realizing the VA case can be "closed" and then need reopening
- Failing to involve the client in records gathering — veterans can access their own records through the VA portal
- Not sorting VA records for accident-relatedness before using them in the demand (VA records include all care, not just MVA-related)
- Circular dependency trap: VA wants treatment dates to reopen case; firm needs records to identify dates. Break this cycle by having the client provide dates or use MyHealtheVet.

# Workers' Compensation Case Patterns

> Workers' compensation cases differ fundamentally from personal injury cases in their procedural framework, involved parties, and resolution mechanisms. WC cases are governed by state Department of Workers' Compensation (DWC) rules rather than civil court procedures, involve employer's WC insurance carriers rather than at-fault BI carriers, and resolve through administrative proceedings (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than jury trials.

## Pattern

### Key Differences from PI Cases
1. **Intake**: CaseOpp system may use the wrong contract type (PI vs WC) — must verify and re-send WC-specific intake documents
2. **Insurance Setup**: LOR sent to WC adjuster (not BI/PIP). DWC insurance lookup required to identify the WC carrier
3. **Treatment Monitoring**: Extended treatment periods common (2+ years). Client may undergo surgery, PT, injections, and still be in active treatment. Treatment includes hardware placement (plates, screws)
4. **Administrative Proceedings**: BRC (Benefit Review Conference) hearings are telephonic, scheduled by DWC, with assigned passcodes. BRCs are frequently rescheduled
5. **Forms**: DWC Form 106 required for various proceedings — sent to client via DocuSign for execution per defense counsel request
6. **Defense Counsel**: Employer/carrier represented by defense firm that exchanges correspondence about Form 106, IME scheduling, and case status
7. **IME**: Scheduled through Commonwealth IME (same service as PI), but invoiced and tracked through WC process. IME is paid by the firm

### WC Case Lifecycle
- Intake & File Setup (WC-specific LOR, DWC carrier lookup)
- Active Treatment (potentially years — surgery, PT, injections, MMI assessment)
- DWC Proceedings (BRC hearings, rescheduling)
- IME / Medical Evaluation
- Defense Counsel Negotiations
- Resolution (settlement or hearing)

## Evidence

- In Case F (WC case, DOI 12/2022), client was injured at a commercial restaurant. The case involved:
  - Intake through CaseOpp with wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring re-send of WC intake docs
  - WC carrier identified via DWC lookup (Accident Fund Insurance)
  - LOR sent to WC adjuster via fax on day 1
  - Extensive treatment: ER visit, orthopedic surgery (plate + 10 screws in wrist), physical therapy, shoulder injections, pain management, MRI, dry needling — spanning 2.5+ years
  - DWC Form 106 sent to client via DocuSign multiple times per defense counsel requests
  - BRC hearings scheduled telephonically, rescheduled multiple times (rescheduled from Feb to Mar 2025)
  - Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) actively exchanged correspondence about IME scheduling and Form 106
  - IME scheduled through Commonwealth IME — coordination via email, invoice tracked
  - WC adjuster communications via email about claim status
  - Case timeline: 3+ years from DOI and still active (Jan 2023 to Mar 2026+)
- In a second WC case (estate claim, DOI 12/2020), the worker died and the estate (represented by the surviving spouse) filed the WC claim. Key observations:
  - The initial claim was denied via DC Form 111 on grounds of "no living person named" and "no supporting documents" — requiring a Motion to Substitute Plaintiff (estate for individual) filed via KY DWC LMS
  - DWC scheduling order assigned an Administrative Law Judge, with BRC hearing and formal hearing dates set
  - Defense counsel filed RFPD (Request for Production of Documents) and contested the claim on multiple grounds
  - Forms 104 and 105 were filed along with 45-Day Disclosures and supporting medical documentation
  - Commonwealth IME was used for medical evaluation (3 separate IME invoices totaling $1,750+), confirming this is the standard WC IME vendor
  - BRC hearings were repeatedly rescheduled over a 3+ year period (DOI 2020, proceedings through 2025)
  - The case resolved through ALJ opinion, which was concurred by the WC Board — demonstrating the full WC administrative resolution pathway
  - LMS (Labor Management System) document notifications were the primary court communication channel, sent via no-reply@ky.gov
  - Total case timeline: 5+ years from DOI (12/2020) through Board opinion (2/2025) — significantly longer than the first observed WC case
  - This case confirms that WC estate claims add procedural complexity (substitution of parties, probate requirements) on top of the already complex WC process
- In a third WC case (workplace injury at automotive manufacturing plant, DOI 5/2023), key observations:
  - WC intake packet sent via VineSign; intake took ~2 months for client to return signed documents
  - The client was receiving TTD (Temporary Total Disability) benefits and still treating for an extended period (2+ years)
  - The firm adopted a "monitoring" posture — simply tracking the client's treatment status rather than actively managing the claim ("Per attorney simply monitoring for client - No active participation - LOR has not been sent")
  - Treatment involved multiple providers including hospital (ER), podiatric surgery, orthopedic specialists, physical therapy, and pain management
  - The WC carrier's defense counsel (O'Bryan, Brown & Toner, PLLC) eventually initiated litigation actions: deposition scheduling (in-person at defense office), IME notice, and Request for Production of Documents
  - Defense counsel requested signed medical release/authorizations specifically for their records collection
  - Deposition was scheduled via defense counsel's legal assistant, with court reporter coordination (Kentuckiana Reporters)
  - **Client death during proceedings**: The client passed away before the scheduled deposition. Family member (mother) called to notify the firm via voicemail. The firm then notified defense counsel immediately.
  - This case illustrates a unique WC outcome — client death requiring procedural pivot (potential estate substitution or claim termination)
- In a fourth WC+BI dual-track case (DOI 6/2021, KY), a Tennessee attorney had settled the WC portion separately (lien resolved for ~$17K) but did not pursue the third-party BI claim. The case was referred to the KY firm on the day before SOL expiration. Key observations:
  - The firm filed a BI complaint in Henry Circuit Court the same day, bypassing the normal case workflow entirely
  - The case was immediately placed into Litigation phase, skipping File Setup, Treatment, and Demand
  - The BI carrier (Old Republic Insurance via Sedgwick TPA) was notified via LOR and complaint
  - The WC carrier (AmTrust North America) had a separate lien of $30,926 on the BI recovery
  - An IME was scheduled through Commonwealth IME — confirming this is the standard vendor for both WC and BI evaluations
  - A telephonic settlement conference occurred approximately 2 years into litigation. The BI carrier's top offer was $35K. The judge indicated recovery above $50K was unlikely
  - The BI carrier paid the WC lien directly ($17K), separate from the BI settlement offer
  - Expert deposition was scheduled via Zoom (Dr. Nazar, orthopedic expert) through Esquire Deposition Solutions
  - A motion to exclude expert testimony was filed by the defense in federal court (transferred from state court)
  - The client received VA treatment at a VA Medical Center — adding VA records collection to the mix alongside WC and BI requirements
  - Case timeline: 4+ years from DOI (6/2021) through active litigation (2025+). This is the longest-running WC+BI case observed
  - This case demonstrates the WC/BI dual-track pattern where the WC settlement creates a lien on the BI recovery, the BI claim is litigated separately, and the BI carrier may pay the WC lien directly as part of settlement negotiations

## Agent Guidance

1. Verify the contract type is WC (not PI) during intake — CaseOpp may default to PI
2. Use DWC insurance lookup to identify the WC carrier
3. Send WC-specific LOR (not PI LOR) to the WC adjuster
4. Track DWC Form 106 requests and ensure client executes via DocuSign promptly
5. Monitor BRC scheduling and rescheduling — update all parties on new dates
6. WC treatment timelines are often much longer than PI — set client expectations accordingly
7. Track IME scheduling and invoicing through the WC process
8. Communicate with defense counsel about Form 106 and IME scheduling per DWC rules
9. Bill review and submission goes to WC carrier, not PIP
10. For estate/death claims: file a Motion to Substitute Plaintiff naming the estate as soon as possible — initial claims may be denied if only the deceased individual is named
11. Track ALJ assignments and hearing dates through the LMS system — expect frequent rescheduling

## Pitfalls

- Using PI intake documents instead of WC-specific forms
- Not performing DWC insurance lookup (WC carriers differ from auto/premises carriers)
- Applying PI case timeline expectations to WC cases (WC cases regularly span 3+ years)
- Missing BRC rescheduling notices (these are time-sensitive)
- Not tracking Form 106 execution status
- Confusing WC process milestones with PI phase transitions
- Filing estate claims in the deceased individual's name instead of the estate's name (immediate denial)
- Not filing Forms 104/105 and 45-Day Disclosures promptly after scheduling order

# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns

> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.

## Pattern

### WC Intake Differences
1. **Employer notification**: Unlike auto accidents, the client must have notified their employer of the workplace injury
2. **WC carrier identification**: The employer's WC carrier (not the client's personal auto/health insurer) is the primary claim target
3. **LOR sent to WC carrier**: Letter of Representation is sent to the WC adjuster via email and fax
4. **Claim acceptance**: The WC adjuster must formally accept the claim before treatment is authorized

### WC Treatment Authorization
Unlike PI cases where the client simply goes to a provider:
1. The WC adjuster must approve the treating provider
2. The firm may need to call the provider to confirm the approval was received
3. If approval is not received by the provider, the firm must fax the approval directly
4. The client's preferred provider may require formal WC approval before scheduling

### TTD (Temporary Total Disability) Benefits
1. TTD payments are periodic wage-replacement benefits during recovery
2. Payments are based on the Average Weekly Wage (AWW)
3. Payment amounts and timing must be tracked — missed or short payments require immediate follow-up with the adjuster
4. AWW disputes may require filing a formal WC claim to get an accurate wage calculation

### AWW (Average Weekly Wage) Disputes
When the employer or carrier calculates the AWW incorrectly:
1. The firm identifies the discrepancy (e.g., from employer records or client-reported wages)
2. A formal WC claim may need to be filed with the state to correct the AWW
3. This affects the TTD rate retroactively — past payments may need adjustment
4. Resolution requires employer wage records and possibly payroll documentation

### WC Deposition and Litigation
WC cases may involve:
- Defense counsel retained by the WC carrier
- Deposition scheduling directly with defense counsel
- Rescheduling is common when defense counsel needs to coordinate

### Employer-Related Evidence
- Screenshots of conversations between client and employer may be relevant
- Employer confusion about the WC claim process is common
- Employer may not directly discuss return-to-work issues, requiring documentation

## Evidence

- In a workplace injury case (elevator incident, DOI 1/2024), the WC adjuster accepted the claim within days. The firm had to fax the treatment approval to the provider because the provider had not received it. TTD payments were tracked over 12+ months, with at least one instance of a missed/short payment requiring follow-up with the adjuster. A formal WC claim was eventually filed because the firm could not verify the accuracy of the AWW. Defense counsel (a national firm) was retained by the carrier, and deposition scheduling involved multiple reschedules over a 2-month period.
- In a WC case (workplace fall at major logistics employer, DOI 2/2025), the employer directed the client to an occupational clinic (Concentra) which only examined one body part despite multiple injuries, issued minimal work restrictions, and told the client they were "not allowed by law to place off work" due to the employer account. The employer failed to initiate a WC claim despite the client being off work for more than 3 days, forcing the client to use vacation time and PTO. The WC carrier (national insurer) eventually assigned an adjuster and defense counsel, but the case demonstrated significant employer resistance to the WC claim process — a pattern distinct from standard WC intake where the carrier promptly accepts the claim.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake for WC cases, confirm: employer name, WC carrier name, adjuster contact, claim number, and whether the employer was notified
2. Send LOR to the WC adjuster immediately — include both email and fax
3. After claim acceptance, confirm the adjuster has authorized the client's chosen provider
4. Track TTD payments: set a recurring task to verify each payment was received on time and at the correct amount
5. If a TTD payment is missed or short, contact the adjuster within 24 hours
6. Collect employer wage records early — AWW disputes are easier to resolve with documentation
7. When defense counsel is assigned, add them to the case contacts immediately
8. For deposition scheduling, coordinate with both the client and defense counsel — expect rescheduling

## Pitfalls

- Assuming WC cases follow the same workflow as auto accident PI cases
- Not confirming treatment authorization was received by the provider (approval may be sent to the firm but not the provider)
- Failing to track TTD payments systematically — missed payments cause client financial distress
- Not filing a formal WC claim when AWW is disputed — the carrier may use an incorrect AWW indefinitely
- Not collecting employer records and screenshots of communications as evidence

# Wrongful Death Case Patterns

> Wrongful death cases differ fundamentally from standard PI cases because the injured party is deceased. The case is brought by a representative (typically a surviving family member) on behalf of the estate. These cases involve no medical treatment records (the decedent was pronounced dead at the scene), but require extensive investigation, accident reconstruction, vocational/economic expert evaluations, and often multi-carrier coordination when multiple at-fault parties or vehicles are involved.

## Pattern

### Key Differences from Standard PI Cases
1. **Client is a representative**: The person the firm communicates with is not the injured party but a surviving family member acting on behalf of the estate
2. **No medical treatment phase**: The decedent has no ongoing treatment, so the case skips the treatment phase entirely and moves from file setup into investigation and litigation
3. **Investigation-heavy**: Wrongful death cases require significant investment in investigation (accident reconstruction, witness interviews, scene evidence) — costs can exceed $20K
4. **Expert evaluations focus on lost earnings**: Vocational economics experts evaluate the decedent's earning capacity and life expectancy rather than treatment outcomes
5. **Multi-carrier complexity**: When multiple vehicles or entities are involved, separate BI claims are filed against each carrier, creating parallel negotiation tracks
6. **Higher litigation costs**: Court reporting, investigation, accident reconstruction, and expert fees are substantially higher than typical PI cases
7. **Settlement release coordination**: Release negotiations involve multiple defense counsel firms and can span months of back-and-forth correspondence
8. **Medicare considerations**: Even in death cases, Medicare questions may arise during settlement if the decedent had Medicare coverage

### Wrongful Death Case Lifecycle
- Intake via representative (surviving family member)
- Insurance claim setup (multiple carriers if applicable)
- Investigation and evidence collection (police reports, accident reconstruction)
- Expert retention (vocational economics, accident reconstruction)
- Complaint filing and litigation
- Discovery and depositions (of representative, witnesses)
- Settlement negotiations (potentially with multiple defense counsel)
- Release execution and disbursement

## Evidence

- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY jurisdiction), the decedent was treated at a university hospital and by EMS but subsequently died. A family member served as primary contact. The case involved a BI claim against a risk management services company and a PIP claim. The firm was communicating through family members (daughter-in-law and husband of the decedent). A large health insurance lien ($89K+) through a union benefits trust was present, requiring calls to the trust's main line and escalation when member status was flagged as "not active" — requiring reinstatement coordination. The firm also retained a collision reconstruction expert. The case involved court filings (circuit and district) with associated filing fees. A lien verification service (Zipliens) was used. The case demonstrates that wrongful death cases from MVAs may still involve substantial hospital treatment and bills (unlike instant-death cases), and that health insurance liens through union/trust benefit plans add another layer of complexity with unique contact and escalation patterns.
- In a wrongful death case (DOI 2/2022, KY jurisdiction), the decedent was pronounced dead at the scene of a multi-vehicle crash. A surviving family member served as representative and primary contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (a personal auto carrier, a commercial trucking company's national liabilities office, and a third personal auto carrier), each requiring independent LOR filing and claim setup. Investigation costs exceeded $20K (focus investigation services). Accident reconstruction ($2K) and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K) were retained. The case was filed in circuit court (Marshall County 23-CI-00073). Court reporter costs across depositions exceeded $1.1K. Additional litigation expenses included multiple defense law firms ($570, $688, $2K, $2.1K). Settlement release negotiations extended over months with back-and-forth emails between the firm and defense counsel. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination. The total case timeline exceeded 3 years from DOI with resolution still pending. Pre-settlement funding ($4K) and workers' compensation lien ($171K+) were also present, adding significant lien complexity. A notary service was used to obtain the representative's signature remotely.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake, identify the representative and their relationship to the decedent — confirm legal authority to act on behalf of the estate
2. Skip the treatment/records collection phase — focus immediately on investigation and insurance claim setup
3. File LORs with all potentially liable carriers within the first week
4. Budget for substantially higher case expenses: investigation ($10K-20K+), accident reconstruction, vocational economics, court reporting
5. Coordinate with multiple defense counsel firms — each carrier typically retains separate counsel
6. Track release negotiations carefully — multiple defense firms means multiple release documents
7. Check for Medicare/Medicaid liens even in death cases — the decedent may have had coverage
8. Maintain regular contact with the representative — they are grieving and need empathetic communication alongside case updates

## Pitfalls

- Treating a wrongful death case like a standard PI case (no treatment phase, investigation-focused)
- Not identifying all potentially liable parties and their carriers early
- Underbudgeting for investigation and expert costs
- Not confirming the representative's legal authority to act for the estate
- Allowing settlement release negotiations to stall without follow-up
- Ignoring Medicare/lien considerations in death cases

## Related

- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — wrongful death cases are almost always litigated
- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — multi-vehicle wrongful death involves parallel carrier tracks
- [[client-communication-through-family]] — the representative is always a family member

# Client Fault Triggers Case Decline

> When police reports or liability investigation reveals the firm's own client was at fault, the case must be declined regardless of the client's injuries or treatment. This is a distinct decline trigger from coverage gaps (no BI/UM) — here the coverage may exist, but there is no viable defendant to pursue. The discovery of client fault may occur weeks or months into file setup, after significant work has already been invested.

## Pattern

The connection works as follows:

1. **Intake** ([[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]]): Client is signed, intake completed, file opened
2. **Police Report** ([[police-records-open-records-requests]]): ORR is submitted; police report may take weeks to months to arrive
3. **Liability Determination**: Police report or adjuster investigation shows client was at fault
4. **Internal Flag**: Paralegal notes "CLIENT AT FAULT" and asks attorney for permission to decline
5. **Decline Decision** ([[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]]): Attorney authorizes decline
6. **Phase Transition** ([[case-phase-transitions]]): Case moves from File Setup to Closed without ever entering Treatment or Demand

## Evidence

- In a case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the paralegal reviewed the accident circumstances and noted "CLIENT AT FAULT" in the file. The paralegal then messaged the attorney: "ok to decline and close? client at fault." This was a PIP-only case where the client had coverage through a small insurer but the liability finding eliminated any third-party recovery.
- In another case (DOI 4/2025, KY), the attorney directed: "double check and see if [client] has any insurance or household insurance. If not set up KAC then decline and close." The liability investigation was still ongoing when the case was being evaluated for viability.

## Insight

Client fault is the fastest path to case decline — it eliminates both BI and UM/UIM recovery paths simultaneously, unlike coverage gaps where PIP or other mechanisms may still provide partial recovery. Firms should prioritize obtaining the police report early in file setup to avoid investing weeks of work on a case that will ultimately be declined due to client fault. However, police reports can take weeks to months, creating a window where work is done on a potentially non-viable case.

## Related

- [[police-records-open-records-requests]] — the ORR that reveals client fault
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — client fault decline skips treatment, demand, and negotiation phases entirely

# Client Frustration Drives Premature Settlement or Attorney Attrition

> When clients become frustrated with the pace and communication during the negotiation phase, two negative outcomes are likely: (1) the client pressures for a premature settlement at a lower value than the case warrants, or (2) the client threatens to or actually changes attorneys. Both outcomes are preventable through proactive communication and clear expectation-setting.

## Connection

**[[client-frustration-escalation]]** documents the 4-stage escalation pattern from polite inquiry to threats and distrust.

**[[client-expectations-management]]** provides the proactive communication strategies that prevent escalation.

**[[negotiation-after-demand]]** shows how premature "settlement" labeling and poor offer communication create client confusion and frustration.

## Insight

In the observed auto accident case, the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" but then refused the offer. Over the following 12+ months, the client made dozens of calls (many missed), sent frustrated text messages, and explicitly threatened to "take another route." Staff members were unsure of the case status ("appears was settled but client didn't accept"). The combination of poor communication, premature settlement labeling, and missed calls created a situation where the client-firm relationship was severely strained.

In the premises liability case (6+ years in litigation), the client texted the attorney directly after a court appearance with no follow-up. The attorney's prompt, substantive response prevented escalation — demonstrating that responsive communication, even when there's limited news, maintains the relationship.

In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the client's frustration was driven by a factor outside the firm's control: another plaintiff's counsel failing to provide medical bills needed for policy limits distribution. The client called repeatedly and was told "we are waiting on the other firm." The frustration escalated to an in-person office visit. This variant shows that inter-counsel coordination delays create the same frustration dynamic as internal delays — and the client cannot distinguish between the two.

In Case N (premises liability estate, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called repeatedly over 18+ months (Jul 2025-Mar 2026) with missed calls and unreturned messages. The emotional stakes are higher in estate cases where the contact is grieving. The long litigation timeline without visible progress compounds the frustration. Despite the firm actively conducting litigation (depositions, discovery), the spouse perceived inaction because he was not being proactively updated.

## Agent Guidance

1. Never label a case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept the offer
2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client within 48 hours to discuss
3. Maintain 2-week check-in schedule during active negotiation
4. When a client shows Stage 2 frustration signs, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
5. Ensure all staff members know the current case status so they can answer client questions coherently

# Coverage Denial Forces Early Litigation and Alternative Recovery Paths

> When a BI carrier denies coverage, the standard case lifecycle is disrupted at the earliest stage. Instead of proceeding through treatment -> demand -> negotiation, the firm must immediately pursue two parallel paths: (1) litigation to challenge the denial or establish liability, and (2) alternative recovery mechanisms (KAC for PIP benefits, UIM for underinsured/uninsured coverage). This dual-path approach significantly increases case complexity and timeline.

## How the Concepts Interact

### Coverage Denial -> Forced Litigation
- [[coverage-denial-and-litigation]]: When BI coverage is denied, the firm cannot send a demand to the carrier. The only path to BI recovery is litigation — filing a complaint against the driver personally and potentially the carrier.
- [[case-phase-transitions]]: The case skips the demand/negotiation phases entirely and moves from file setup directly to litigation.
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]]: The SOL clock runs regardless of coverage disputes. The firm must file within 2 years even if coverage is still "under investigation."

### Alternative Recovery Paths
- [[kentucky-assigned-claims-process]]: When no BI coverage exists, KAC provides PIP-style benefits through an assigned carrier. This runs as a separate track from the litigation.
- [[uim-claim-management]]: If the client has UIM coverage, a UIM claim provides another recovery path independent of the BI coverage dispute.

### Cascade Effect
In observed cases:
1. BI denial (Day 0) -> Attorney challenge (Weeks 1-8) -> Coverage still under investigation (Month 3-6)
2. KAC claim filed (Month 1) -> Assigned carrier manages PIP benefits (ongoing)
3. Medicaid/health insurance lien grows as treatment continues (ongoing)
4. Complaint filed to preserve SOL (Month 13)
5. Defense counsel extension granted to resolve coverage (Month 15)
6. EUO of defendant (Month 18+)
7. Full litigation (discovery, depositions) while coverage still disputed (Year 2-4+)

The result: cases that should resolve in 12-18 months instead take 4+ years. Client frustration compounds as bills go to collections, Medicaid liens grow, and the case shows no visible progress.

## Evidence

- In Case H, the BI carrier (Allstate) denied coverage due to a stolen vehicle claim. The firm challenged the denial with evidence that the theft report was filed after the accident. Despite this, coverage remained "under investigation" for 6+ months. The firm filed a complaint (22-CI-002878) at 13 months. A KAC claim was set up through Nationwide for PIP benefits. Defense counsel was granted a 60-day extension while attempting to resolve coverage. An EUO of the defendant was conducted. The case remained in active litigation 4+ years later. The Conduent (Medicaid) lien reached $20,669.08.
- In Case G, while not a coverage denial per se, the at-fault driver's BI policy limits were insufficient, requiring a UIM claim against the client's own carrier (Allstate). The UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580) approximately 14 months after the accident. This demonstrates the alternative-recovery-path pattern: when BI is insufficient (or denied), the firm must pursue the client's own carrier through UIM litigation.

## Agent Guidance

1. When BI coverage is denied, immediately initiate three parallel workstreams:
   - Litigation track (complaint preparation, SOL monitoring)
   - KAC/alternative PIP track (file KAC application, track assigned carrier)
   - UIM evaluation (check client's own UIM coverage limits)
2. Track all three workstreams independently with separate follow-up schedules
3. Set client expectations for a 3-5+ year timeline
4. Monitor Medicaid/health insurance lien growth throughout — these liens compound over time
5. Brief the attorney on the full picture: BI denial + KAC status + UIM potential + lien exposure


- In a file-setup case (DOI 10/2025), coverage was denied for both vehicles after investigation by Liberty Mutual. Unlike the stolen-vehicle case that led to litigation, this case had no treatment and no PIP — making litigation impractical. The coverage denial here leads to case decline rather than forced litigation, representing an alternate outcome path: when coverage denial meets zero treatment, the case simply dies rather than entering forced litigation.

## Related

- [[uim-claim-management]] — the UIM alternative recovery path
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — coverage denial disrupts normal phase flow
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL creates hard deadlines regardless of coverage status
